title: Dog Head Pressing Against Wall: Causes and Emergency Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog pressing their head against a wall? This is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what it means and why you need a vet immediately.
content_html:
What is Head Pressing?

Head pressing is a clinical sign where a dog compulsively pushes the top of their head against a wall, floor, or other hard surface. This behavior should not be confused with 'head butting' for affection; it is a neurological symptom indicating that the dog is in distress or experiencing pressure within the brain.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for head pressing is High. This behavior is typically a sign of serious neurological damage or dysfunction affecting the forebrain or brain stem. Common underlying causes can include lead poisoning, liver shunts, brain tumors, or stroke. If you observe your dog doing this, you must seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency animal hospital.

How Photos and Videos Help Triage

It is highly recommended that you take a quick video or photo of your dog performing this behavior before heading to the clinic. Because dogs may stop head pressing due to the adrenaline or stress of a car ride or a new environment, having visual evidence allows your veterinarian to confirm the symptom quickly and begin life-saving diagnostics without delay.

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as a dog resting its head?
faqs.answer: No. Resting is a relaxed state. Head pressing is a compulsive, repetitive, or sustained behavior where the dog appears unable to stop pushing against a firm object.

faqs.question: Can liver disease cause head pressing?
faqs.answer: Yes. A condition called hepatic encephalopathy, which occurs when the liver cannot filter toxins from the blood, can lead to neurological symptoms including head pressing.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for circling, changes in vision, seizures, or sudden behavioral changes, as these often accompany head pressing in neurological emergencies.
